description_long: This object is a robust, irregularly shaped guard stone, hewn from a single block of coarse stone. Its surface is rough and weathered, with visible chisel marks and natural fractures, giving it a rugged, utilitarian appearance. The stone is predominantly a muted gray-brown, with subtle variations in tone due to mineral content and exposure to the elements. One side features a distinct, angular notch or step, likely designed to interlock with adjacent stones or to provide a barrier edge. The overall form is asymmetrical, with a broad, flat top and a slightly tapered base, suggesting it was intended to be partially embedded in the ground for stability. This type of guard stone would typically be found at the edge of a street, driveway, or building corner, serving to protect structures from vehicle impact.
description: A rugged, gray-brown stone guard with a rough, weathered surface and an angular notch, designed to serve as a protective barrier in urban settings.
description_view_0: The front view shows the broad, flat face of the guard stone with its rough, uneven texture and rounded edges.
description_view_1: This side view highlights the angular notch cut into the stone, as well as the sloped, chiseled surface.
description_view_2: The rear view reveals the tapered end and the irregular, fractured base, emphasizing the stone's asymmetry.
description_view_3: This side view displays the full length of the guard stone and the depth of the notch, with visible surface roughness and chipping.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the edge of urban streets, driveways, or building corners, often near the entrance to courtyards or at the base of historical structures to prevent vehicle encroachment.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be partially embedded in the ground with the flat or notched side facing outward, fully above ground or flush with the surface for maximum barrier effect.
attribute_wear_patterns: Visible chipping and smoothing on the edges and corners, likely caused by repeated vehicle contact and foot traffic. Surface discoloration and minor erosion are present due to prolonged exposure to weather elements, with the top and outward-facing surfaces most affected.
